# Dr Yellapragada Subbarao

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!o9aZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffd9cb0c6-c64d-439c-8ada-8d7b56aa1faa_602x768.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!o9aZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ffd9cb0c6-c64d-439c-8ada-8d7b56aa1faa_602x768.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

*“You’ve probably never heard of Dr. Yellapragada Subbarow. Yet because he lived you may be alive and are well today. Because he lived you may live longer.” — Doron Antrim, 1950*

Dr. Yellapragada Subbarao’s legacy is one of brilliance, resilience, and quiet revolution in the world of medicine. Born on January 12, 1895 — the same date as Swami Vivekananda — his life mirrored Swamiji’s in its transformative impact, albeit in the realm of science rather than spirituality. His discoveries have saved millions, and his legacy deserves to be etched alongside the likes of Alexander Fleming and Ronald Ross.

Dr. Subbarao’s legacy came full circle in 1994, when India faced a deadly outbreak of plague in Surat and parts of Maharashtra. In what was the centenary year of his birth, the very antibiotics he helped pioneer — tetracyclines, specifically Doxycycline, a third-generation variant — played a crucial role in combating the epidemic.

The plague struck between August and October 1994, with over 693 suspected cases and 56 deaths reported across several states. Doxycycline, derived from the tetracycline family that Subbarow’s research helped establish, was one of the key antibiotics used to control the outbreak. That this life-saving drug helped India in the year marking Subbarao’s 100th birth anniversary felt like a poetic tribute — his science returning home to serve the land he loved.

Dr. Yellapragada Subbarao was not just a scientific genius,he was a staunch nationalist, a devout Gandhian, and a man of unshakable principle. His choice to wear a Khadi surgical gown at Madras Medical College, in response to Gandhi’s Swadeshi call, was more than symbolic ,it was a bold act of defiance against colonial conformity.

His refusal to wear British surgical attire and his choice of Khadi earned him the ire of Professor M.C. Bradfield. As a result, he was awarded the LMS (Licentiate in Medicine and Surgery) instead of the full MBBS, barring him from entering the Madras Medical Service.

He joined Dr. Lakshmipathi’s Ayurvedic College in Madras as an anatomy lecturer. It was here that he developed a deep interest in Ayurveda, inspired by its healing potential and the possibility of integrating it with modern science.

With financial help from his father-in-law and the Malladi Satyalingam Naicker Charities, he reached Boston in 1922. He was admitted to Harvard Medical School under the designation of “physician,” though his qualifications were listed as “chemist” ,a workaround necessitated by U.S. immigration laws that allowed Indians entry only under those two categories.

Despite holding a valid student visa, Subbarao lived under the constant fear of deportation. At the time, Indians were not allowed to become U.S. citizens, even if they met all legal requirements. He supported himself by working menial jobs ,while conducting groundbreaking research at Harvard.

After earning a diploma from Harvard School of Tropical Medicine, Subbarow was appointed only as a junior faculty member at Harvard Medical School. Despite lacking an MBBS, denied to him in India for wearing Khadi,he collaborated with Dr. Cyrus Fiske to develop the Fiske–Subbarow Method, a groundbreaking technique for estimating phosphorus in body fluids.

This work led to the discovery of phosphocreatine and adenosine triphosphate (ATP) — the molecules that power every cell in the human body. These discoveries earned him citations in biochemistry textbooks worldwide.

In the 1920s, as scientists puzzled over how the body stores and utilizes energy from food, it was Subbarao,working with Cyrus Fiske at Harvard, who cracked the code.

*How does the body store energy from food, and how is it accessed when needed?*

It led to discovery of two key molecules, Phosphocreatine, A rapid energy reservoir in muscle cells and Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP): The universal energy currency of the cell.When energy is needed, ATP is converted into ADP (Adenosine Diphosphate), releasing energy for cellular processes. This cycle is fundamental to everything from muscle contraction to brain activity.Their work highlighted the critical role of phosphorus in energy metabolism and cellular function.

The Fiske-Subbarow method was Developed to quantify inorganic phosphorus in biological samples. It involved creating a protein-free filtrate from blood, reacting it with ammonium molybdate and perchloric acid, forming phosphomolybdic acid.

This is then reduced using 1,2,4-amino naphthol sulfonic acid (ANSA) to produce a blue compound, measured colorimetrically at 620 nm. Despite newer techniques, this method remains a standard in biochemistry labs worldwide.

After being denied a regular faculty position at Harvard despite his groundbreaking work, Dr. Subbarao moved to Lederle Laboratories in 1940, then a division of American Cyanamid (now part of Wyeth, owned by Pfizer). It was here that he entered what many consider the most productive phase of his career.

Subbarow recognized the limitations of existing antibiotics like penicillin and streptomycin, which were effective only against specific bacteria. He envisioned broad-spectrum antibiotics that could treat a wider range of infections.

Under his leadership, polymyxin was developed — a potent antibiotic still used today, especially in veterinary medicine and cattle feed. It targets Gram-negative bacteria and remains vital in treating drug-resistant infections.

In 1945, under Subbarow’s guidance, Benjamin Minge Duggar discovered chlortetracycline, the first tetracycline antibiotic. Marketed as Aureomycin, it became one of the most versatile antibiotics of its time, revolutionizing treatment for respiratory, urinary, and intestinal infections.

Though Dr.Subbarao didn’t personally isolate these antibiotics, he led the research teams, set the scientific direction, and created an environment where such discoveries could flourish. His leadership at Lederle turned the lab into a powerhouse of pharmaceutical innovation.

In 1945, Subbaro and his team at Lederle successfully synthesized folic acid from liver and microbial sources.

This discovery was pivotal in treating tropical sprue, a debilitating disease that had taken the lives of two of his brothers within just eight days , a loss that haunted him and fueled his research.Folic acid (Vitamin B9) became a cornerstone in the treatment of anemia and malnutrition, especially in tropical regions.

Doxycycline, a third-generation tetracycline, was developed under Subbarao’s antibiotic program at Lederle.It was later approved as a preventive drug for malaria, used widely by travelers and military personnel in endemic regions.This drug also played a crucial role in controlling the 1994 Surat plague.

One of the most important anti cancer drugs methotrexate was synthesized again by Dr. Subbarao .It is used to treat Burkitt’s Lymphoma, a fast-growing cancer of the lymphatic system,Childhood leukemia,various adult cancers and Autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis.Methotrexate remains a cornerstone drug in oncology and immunology even today.

Subbarao also discovered diethylcarbamazine (DEC), marketed as Hetrazan, the only effective drug for treating filariasis, a parasitic disease that causes elephantiasis.The World Health Organization (WHO) adopted Hetrazan as a key element in its Global Programme to Eliminate Lymphatic Filariasis, which has reached over 657 million people across 39 countries.

Dr. Yellapragada Subbarao passed away on August 8, 1948, in New York City at the age of 53, just a year after India gained independence. His death marked the end of a life that had already transformed global medicine, but also left behind the poignant question: *What more could he have given, had he returned to his homeland?*

There are accounts suggesting that Dr. Subbarao was planning to return to India, possibly to contribute to its newly independent scientific and medical landscape. His deep nationalist roots and Gandhian values made this a natural aspiration.

He embodied the spirit of a Karmayogi — one who performs his duty with dedication and detachment. He never sought fame or accolades, only the fulfillment of his vow to his mother: *“I must win a name in the world, and then only would life be worthwhile.”*

Dr. Subbarao’s journey is a powerful reminder that greatness isn’t always recognized in its time. He wasn’t an MBBS. He wasn’t a tenured professor. But he was a scientific visionary whose discoveries continue to save lives.

Despite his monumental contributions to global medicine, his name is rarely mentioned in Indian textbooks, public discourse, or national honors.

Unlike other scientific pioneers, Dr. Subbarow was never awarded a Padma or the Bharat Ratna, despite discoveries that rival those of Alexander Fleming and Ronald Ross. Outside the medical and scientific community, his name remains largely unknown. His contributions — ATP, methotrexate, folic acid, Hetrazan — are taught, but rarely attributed to him.
